football_analytics.title: Football Analytics
football_analytics.description: Exploratory and statistical analysis of the Premier League.

correlations.theory:
### Correlations between match metrics
The **Pearson correlation coefficient** (r) measures the linear relationship between two variables and ranges from **−1 to 1**: near 1 both grow together, near −1 one rises as the other falls, and near 0 there is barely any linear relationship. Each cell crosses two metrics computed per team and match. The color scale is **diverging** (blue = positive, red = negative) so it reads well without relying on red-green. Switch the scope to compare **the whole league** with the **Top 6** subset.

inference.theory:
### Is there a home advantage?
We apply a one-sided **Student's t-test (Welch)** to check whether home teams outperform away teams:

- **H₀**: the home mean equals the away mean.
- **H₁**: the home mean is **greater** than the away mean.

The **t-statistic** measures how many standard errors separate the two means; the **p-value** is the probability of observing that difference (or a larger one) if H₀ were true. If the p-value falls below **α = 0.05** we reject H₀. The chart shows the **sampling distribution** of each mean: the less the bell curves overlap, the clearer the difference.
inference.story_significant: **Verdict.** With a t-statistic = **{t_stat}** and a p-value of **{p_value}** (well below 0.05), the null hypothesis is rejected. The difference in {metric} between the home mean (**{mean_home}**) and the away mean (**{mean_away}**) —about **{diff}** of advantage— **is not due to chance**: the home advantage is statistically confirmed, at Riazor… I mean, in the Premier. 🔵⚪
inference.story_not_significant: **Verdict.** With t = **{t_stat}** and a p-value of **{p_value}** (above 0.05), there is **not** enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis. The difference in {metric} between home (**{mean_home}**) and away (**{mean_away}**), of **{diff}**, **could be due to chance**. This time not even the Riazor factor saves the test. 🔵⚪

montecarlo.theory:
### Monte Carlo simulation (Dixon-Coles model)
The **Dixon-Coles** model assigns each team two parameters estimated by **maximum likelihood**: its **attacking strength** and its **defensive weakness**. The goals in each match are modeled with a **Poisson distribution** whose mean depends on the attack of the scorer, the defense of the conceder and a **home-advantage** parameter.

With those parameters we **replay the season thousands of times**, generating random scorelines. Aggregating all simulations yields, for each team, its probability of being **champion**, finishing in the **Top 4** or being **relegated**, thereby separating real **quality** from **luck**.
montecarlo.placeholder: Press **Simulate season** to generate the league's predictive table.
montecarlo.note: > Simulation based on the **Dixon-Coles** model: each team has an attacking strength and a defensive weakness estimated by maximum likelihood, and playing at home multiplies the expected goals by a factor of **{factor}**.
montecarlo.story: After **{n_sims}** simulated seasons, **{champion}** is crowned champion in **{champion_prob}%** of the parallel universes, with **{runner_up}** as the main challenger. At the other end, **{relegated}** suffers the most: relegated in **{relegation_prob}%** of the simulations. Quality rules, but luck always has the final word.

supervised.title: Supervised Learning
supervised.description: Match outcome prediction (1/X/2) with a supervised classification engine: stratified cross-validation and strict data-leakage control.
supervised.theory: We predict the **outcome of each match** in the 2022-23 Premier League (**1** home · **X** draw · **2** away) using *only pre-kickoff information*. Since the final scoreline determines the result and the remaining statistics are generated **during** the game, using them as input would be data leakage. That is why each match is described by the **rolling averages of each team's 5 previous matches** (form, goals, shots, corners, fouls, cards…) and their differentials. The engine follows a rigorous evaluation protocol: **stratified 10-fold cross-validation**, **Z-score standardization computed exclusively on each fold's training set** (no leakage) and a **fixed seed** for full reproducibility. Each model family is tuned over a **bounded, predefined hyperparameter grid**.
supervised.baseline_hint: No hyperparameters: default logistic regression (L2, C=1.0, lbfgs).
supervised.robustness_intro: With just 10 accuracy values (one per fold) we compare models rigorously. **Shapiro-Wilk** tests whether those 10 values follow a normal distribution (*p > 0.05* → normal). The **Wilcoxon** signed-rank test compares two models pairwise over the **same folds**: if *p ≤ 0.05* the difference is significant; otherwise they are statistically equivalent and **Occam's Razor** comes into play.
supervised.compare_prompt: Choose a rival for the test
supervised.story_intro: **{model}** reaches a **mean accuracy of {accuracy}%** (error {error}%, macro F1 {f1}) under 10-fold cross-validation. As a reference, always predicting the majority class would give {baseline}%. In football the predictability ceiling is low, so these values are the expected ones: a match result has a huge random component.
supervised.story_overfit: ⚠️ **Overfitting detected.** The best configuration was **{best}** ({best_acc}%); by increasing {param} to **{complex}** accuracy drops to {complex_acc}%. The model starts to **memorize noise** instead of learning the general pattern — the *curse of dimensionality* in action: more complexity does not imply better generalization.
supervised.story_confusion: **Reading the matrix:** the most frequent error is {n_err} matches of type **{real_cls}** predicted as **{pred_cls}**. The class with the worst accuracy is **{worst_cls}**; in practice **draws** are the hardest to anticipate because they barely leave a trace in teams' prior form.
supervised.story_significant: The **Wilcoxon test (p = {p})** indicates a **significant difference**: **{better}** consistently outperforms **{worse}** across the 10 folds. The improvement is not down to chance.
supervised.story_equivalent: **Wilcoxon (p = {p})**: there is no significant difference between **{model_a}** and **{model_b}**. For this problem they perform statistically equivalently.
supervised.story_ockham: **Occam's Razor.** With **Wilcoxon p = {p}** there is no significant difference between **{complex}** and **{simple}**. Given equivalent performance, it is best to keep **{simple}**: simpler, more interpretable and cheaper to train than **{complex}**.

unsupervised.title: Unsupervised Learning
unsupervised.description: Discovering playing styles (profiles) of the 20 teams through PCA and clustering.
unsupervised.theory: There are no labels here: we let the data reveal **playing styles**. We summarize each of the 20 teams' season into **5 advanced KPIs** — attacking volume (shots on target), efficiency (goals per shot on target), defensive solidity, game control (corners) and aggressiveness (fouls + cards) — standardize them with **Z-score** and run **exhaustive experimentation**: **PCA** to see how much information fits into few dimensions, and three *clustering* families (**K-Means**, **hierarchical** and **SOM**) evaluated with the **Silhouette Score**. The scatter projects the teams onto the first 2 principal components; the radar shows each profile's DNA.
unsupervised.pca_panel_title: Academic rigor · PCA components by explained variance
unsupervised.run_prompt: Configure an algorithm and press Run to discover the playing profiles.

quality_phrase.weak: a weak structure, with overlap between profiles
quality_phrase.poor: a loose grouping: the profiles overlap quite a bit (natural with only 20 teams and continuously varying styles)

unsupervised.story_intro: **{algo} · {config}** splits the 20 teams into **{n} playing profiles**.
unsupervised.story_silhouette: Clustering quality — **Silhouette = {sil}**: {quality}. The Silhouette measures how compact and well-separated the groups are (1 = perfect · 0 = overlapping · negative = misassigned).
unsupervised.story_silhouette_na: With this grid the SOM assigns almost one team per neuron (**over-segmentation**): the Silhouette is undefined. Reduce the grid size (e.g. 4×4) to obtain groupable profiles — it is the method's lesson: a grid too large for only 20 teams does not generalize.
unsupervised.story_cluster_both: **{name}** ({size} teams · {teams}) — excels in **{high}** and falls short in **{low}**.
unsupervised.story_cluster_balanced: **{name}** ({size} teams · {teams}) — a balanced profile, with no extreme traits.

knowledge_hub.title: DataMill Analytics
knowledge_hub.subtitle: Technical whitepaper of the analytics engine
knowledge_hub.lead: Technical documentation of the product: from raw data to **outcome prediction** and **playing-style discovery**. Architecture, methodology and engine stack.
knowledge_hub.sec1_body:
**DataMill Analytics** is a professional football analytics engine that applies *exhaustive machine learning* to the 2022-23 Premier League with a dual goal: **predicting match outcomes** (supervised learning) and **discovering teams' playing styles** (unsupervised learning).

Each module replicates a rigorous research methodology —closed grid search, cross-validation and statistical testing— and exposes it through a reactive, multilingual and accessible interface. It is not a demo: it is an end-to-end reproducible *pipeline*, with the mathematics isolated from the front-end and results cached for fluid exploration.
knowledge_hub.sec2_body:
#### Data leakage prevention
The final result (FTR) is determined by the scoreline, and the match statistics (shots, corners, fouls…) are generated **during** the game. Using them as input would be catastrophic leakage. That is why, for each match, the **rolling averages of each team's 5 previous matches** are computed (recent form, goals, shots on target, corners, cards…) along with their differentials. Intra-match information only feeds the history; it **never** enters as a predictor.

#### Evaluated algorithms
Six families trained via **closed grid search** (no open ranges):
- **Baseline** — Logistic Regression (L2).
- **ANN** — 8 topologies of 1-2 hidden layers.
- **SVM** — 8 configurations (linear, polynomial g=3 and RBF).
- **Decision Trees** — depth 2 to 7.
- **k-NN** — odd k from 1 to 11.
- **DoME (proxy)** — Random Forest bounded by number of nodes.

#### Statistical rigor
- **Stratified 10-fold cross-validation**, with a fixed seed for total reproducibility.
- **Strict per-fold Z-score standardization**: mean and standard deviation are computed only on each partition's *train* set (zero leakage).
- **Shapiro-Wilk test** on the 10 accuracies (normality) and **Wilcoxon signed-rank** for pairwise comparison. When two models are statistically equivalent, **Occam's Razor** applies: the simpler, more interpretable one is favored.
knowledge_hub.sec3_body:
The *Team Profiling* module summarizes each team's season into **5 advanced KPIs** —attacking volume, efficiency, defensive solidity, game control and aggressiveness— and standardizes them with Z-score.

#### Pipeline
- **PCA** — principal component analysis: it reports how many components are needed to explain from 70 to 95 % of the variance, and projects the teams into 2D (PC1/PC2) for visualization.
- **K-Means** — k from 2 to 7.
- **Hierarchical clustering** — via `scipy.cluster.hierarchy` (`linkage` + `fcluster`), with the *ward, complete, average, single* methods and **centroid**.
- **SOM networks** — *Self-Organizing Maps* (`minisom`) over 6 symmetric high-dimensional grids, from 4×4 to 10×10.

#### Evaluation
The quality of each grouping is measured with the **Silhouette Score**. The system explicitly handles **over-segmentation**: when a large SOM grid assigns almost one team per neuron, the Silhouette becomes undefined and is reported as such, instead of fabricating a value.
knowledge_hub.sec4_body:
The software core combines a robust mathematical *back-end* with a fully reactive *front-end*:

- **Python** as the base language.
- **Dash** and **Plotly** for the reactive interface and the interactive visualizations (PCA scatter, radars, *heatmaps*, Monte Carlo simulations).
- **scikit-learn**, **SciPy** and **MiniSom** as the *machine learning* engine (supervised models, PCA, hierarchical clustering and SOM).

**Custom i18n architecture.** Multi-language support is **native**, not a patch: a custom *translator* with hierarchical keys, cascading *fallback* and dynamic formatting serves the entire application in **6 languages** with no external dependencies. The interface also follows accessibility principles and a **colorblind-safe palette** (CVD-safe).
knowledge_hub.sec5_body:
**Brais Muiño Otero** — *Lead Data Scientist & Engineer*

Full design, development and implementation of the project: data engineering, *machine learning* engine, application architecture, visualization and internationalization.

🔗 **GitHub:** [github.com/brais-muino-otero](https://github.com/brais-muino-otero)  ·  **Live demo:** [huggingface.co/spaces/Braizito/datamill-analytics](https://huggingface.co/spaces/Braizito/datamill-analytics)
